coredata.dc:title: Cubic carbon quantum dots for light-harvesters in mesoscopic solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Dou, Dawei
Duan, Jialong
Zhao, Yuanyuan
He, Benlin
Tang, Qunwei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials that have emerged recently and have garnered much interest in many fields. We present here a simple hydrothermal conversion from Gelidium amansii powders, a type of red algae, to cubic carbon quantum dots (CCQDs). The synthesized CCQDs exist in average size of 4.87 nm, and have fluorescence quantum yield of 3.12% as well as good water solubility. CCQDs are applied as light-harvesters for mesoscopic solar cells. The solar cell tailored with CCQDs/N719 yields a maximized power conversion efficiency of 8.68% in comparison to 7.83% for N719-only device. We anticipate that our results will be a vital point for further research, leading to high-performance next-generation solar cell.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cubic carbon quantum dot
Mesoscopic solar cell
Light harvester
Solar energy utilization

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21503202 and 61774139 ), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( JQ201714 ) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 201762018 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Hydrothermal conversion from Gelidium amansii powers to CCQDs. (b) The typical TEM image of CCQDs at high-resolution upon hydrothermal reaction for 6 h. The inset shows the FFT image of CCQDs. (c) The size distribution of CCQDs. (d) The line-profile analysis of the CCQDs.

(a) PL emission spectra of the CCQDs synthesized at a heating time of 6 h under different excitation wavelengths. (b) Color coordinates of the fabricated CCQDs. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of CCQDs synthesized at a heating time of 6 h (d) FTIR spectrum of CCQDs synthesized at a heating time of 6 h. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Schematic diagram of a mesoscopic solar cell tailored with CCQDs and N719 dye. (b) The energy level distribution and charge transfer within the photoanode. (c) CV curve of CCQDs at a heating time of 6 h. (d) Characteristic photo J-V curves of solar cells with only CCQDs under simulated solar irradiation (100 mW cm−2, AM 1.5). (e) Characteristic photo J-V curves of solar cells with N719/CCQDs.
(a) Nyquist and (b) Bode EIS phase plots of the solar cells.

The photovoltaic parameters of MSCs with CCQDs-only, N719-only and CCQDs/N719 as light harvesters. η: power conversion efficiency; J sc: short-circuit current density; V oc: open-circuit voltage; FF: fill factor.

head.ce:title: Cubic carbon quantum dots for light-harvesters in mesoscopic solar cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials that have emerged recently and have garnered much interest in many fields. We present here a simple hydrothermal conversion from Gelidium amansii powders, a type of red algae, to cubic carbon quantum dots (CCQDs). The synthesized CCQDs exist in average size of 4.87 nm, and have fluorescence quantum yield of 3.12% as well as good water solubility. CCQDs are applied as light-harvesters for mesoscopic solar cells. The solar cell tailored with CCQDs/N719 yields a maximized power conversion efficiency of 8.68% in comparison to 7.83% for N719-only device. We anticipate that our results will be a vital point for further research, leading to high-performance next-generation solar cell.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the first finding as an unknown fluorescent carbon nanomaterial [1], carbon quantum dot (CQD) has been a new member of carbonaceous material with a lateral size below 10 nm. The weak luminescence and the limited solubility markedly restrict the widespread application of general carbonaceous materials. On the contrary, CQDs have good water solubility and bright fluorescence, making them promising for solution-processable fields ranging from biomedical imaging [2], displays to energy conversion devices [3]. Upon excitation by sunlight, CQDs absorb wide-spectral light and release photogenerated electrons, providing new opportunities in optoelectronics, catalysis and sensing and other fields [4]. Recently, great achievements have been made using single-CQDs as light-harvesters for photovoltaics or combination with organic dyes to widen spectral-response range [5–7]. The high dependence of energy-level structures on surface energy states, sizes, shapes, number of layers calls for optimal synthesis of CQDs from either bottom-up or top-down methods.

Until now, the prestigious bottom-up method for CQDs synthesis has involved conversion from polycyclic aromatic molecules [8,9] or other compounds with aromatic structures [10,11]. The synthesis procedures for CQDs are complicated and the shapes are always spherical dots using crops, fruits, and vegetables etc. as raw materials. The single shape has been a limitation for specific-requirement performances. Here we present the rapid conversion from cost-effective and environmental friendliness gelidium amansii to large-scale cubic carbon quantum dots (CCQDs) within several hours and illustrate their applications in solar cells. Gelidium amansii, a type of red algae, belongs to the hi-negative plants and grows on the rocks of shallow sea. The polysaccharides in gelidium amansii are decorated with gigantic oxygenic groups, therefore the final CCQDs are also expected to have extraordinary energy-level structures and photo-excited performances in photovoltaic devices. Mesoscopic solar cells (MSCs) attract growing interests and experience persistent efficiency rise through breakthroughs in key material innovation, structure reorganization, and technology optimization [12]. However, CQD-only tailored MSCs yield lower power conversion efficiency (PCE) because of the weak affinity between CQDs and mesoscopic TiO2 and narrow spectral response [13,14]. One of the solutions to this challenge is to combine CQDs with organic dyes. Our results have shown that the PCE of photovoltaic device is markedly enhanced using CCQDs/N719-dye as light-harvesters, arising from widened light absorption ranging from visible to near-infrared regions as well as fast hole extraction for efficient electron-hole separation [15]. This study will focus on the preparation of CCQDs and efficiency enhancement through co-sensitization by N719 and CCQDs.

The conversion of CCQDs from Gelidium amansii was realized through a simple hydrothermal process. Under vigorous agitation, 1.0 g of Gelidium amansii powders were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water and subsequently transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The reaction was maintained at 170 °C for 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 and 12 h to obtain CCQDs aqueous solutions. Subsequently, the CCQDs were stored at room temperature for use.

The detailed procedures for preparing TiO2 colloid were similar to our previous report [16]. Mesoscopic TiO2 (m-TiO2) films with size of 5 mm2 were made by coating TiO2 colloid onto freshly cleaned FTO glass substrates by a doctor-blade method and then calcined at 450 °C for 30 min in air. The resultant m-TiO2 electrodes were immersed in concentrated CCQDs aqueous solutions for 10 h to obtain CCQDs-TiO2 photoanodes.

The m-TiO2 anodes were immersed into a 0.50 mM N719 ethanol solution for 24 h to obtain N719-TiO2 photoanodes. Then the N719-TiO2 photoanodes were spin-coated by CCQDs ethyl alcohol solution for 30 s at a speed of 2000 rpm to obtain CCQDs/N719-TiO2 photoanodes.

The pyrolyzed platinum electrode was used as a counter electrode. Each solar cell device was built by sandwiching a I−/I3 − redox electrolyte between a platinum counter electrode and a photoanode and sealed with a Surlyn film (30 μm in thickness). A liquid electrolyte consisting of 0.1 M tetraethylammonium iodide, 0.1 M tetramethylammonium iodide, 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium iodide, 0.1 M NaI, 0.1 M KI, 0.1 M LiI, 0.05 M I2 and 0.05 M 4-tert-butyl-pyridine in acetonitrile was used.

The energy levels of CCQDs at a heating time of 6 h were measured by a cyclic voltammetry (CV) method using a standard three-electrode system, consisting of a working electrode of platinum foil supported CCQDs, a counter electrode of platinum wire, a reference electrode of Ag/AgCl, and a supporting electrolyte of 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate in acetonitrile. The CV curve was scanned from −1.5 to +1.5 V at a scanning rate of 50 mV s−1. The lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) levels are calculated according to E LUMO = -e (E red + 4.4) V and E HOMO = -e (E ox + 4.4) V [17].

The photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) curves of solar cells with an active area of 5 mm2 were recorded on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation under irradiation of simulated solar light from a 100 W xenon arc lamp in ambient atmosphere. The incident light intensity was controlled to 100 mW cm−2 (calibrated by using a standard silicon solar cell). The photovoltaic performances have been repeated at least 30 times to eliminate experimental errors.

Using Gelidium amansii powders as raw materials, CCQDs can be facilely synthesized by a simple hydrothermal process. Fig. 1 a shows the conversion of carbohydrates to CCQDs as well as corresponding transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of monodispersed CCQDs, generating bright emission colors under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation and an average diameter size of 3–7 nm. The reaction temperature is controlled at 170 °C by tuning reaction time from 2 to 12 h for performance optimization. The mechanism behind this conversion is that the polysaccharides such as starch in Gelidium amansii hydrolyze into glucose (Fig. S1), and subsequently polymerize into CQDs through dehydration reactions [18]. The corresponding CQDs are subjected to TEM characterization, as shown in Fig. 1b, suggesting a cubic shape. A possible reason for geometric CCQDs may result from “soft template” function of proteins in Gelidium amansii, and the dehydration processes are performed in these cubic micelles. The micelles reduce surface energy and therefore stabilize CCQDs. An in-plane lattice spacing of 0.233 nm is detected from the high resolution TEM image, which is similar to previous report [19]. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) image (insert in Fig. 1b) cross-checks a quadrangle structure for as-prepared CCQDs. The counted size distribution obeys a Gaussian mode, as shown in Fig. 1c, and the main size distribution region is 4.5–5.5 nm, suggesting that the hydrothermal method proposed in the current work benefits the mono-dispersed CCQDs. From the line-profile analysis in Fig. 1d one can find that the average interplane spacing of CCQDs agrees well with HRTEM characterization. Our results demonstrate CQDs with cubic shape and homogeneous size distribution can be prepared using the method reported here.

Fig. S2a shows the images of CCQDs solutions as a function of heating time from 2 to 12 h. Apparently, the color darkens with increase of heating time, suggesting an increased CCQDs concentration. Upon excitation by UV light, as shown in Fig. S2b, the CCQDs emit green photoluminescence. There is no obvious change in emission color at different heating time but the solution at 6 h is the brightest, referring to a maximized photoexcitation behavior. Fig. 2 a shows photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the corresponding CCQDs solutions by tuning excitation wavelengths (λ ex) from 330 to 510 nm. The PL intensity and maximal emission wavelengths (λ em) are highly dependent on λ ex. There are no emission peaks under excitation at λ ex < 350 nm, and a maximized PL intensity is determined at λ ex = 470 nm. The emission-dependent behavior arises decoration of CCQDs by oxygenic groups (OH, CO, CO, C O C, etc), forming surface states energy levels between π and π* states of CC segments [20]. When suffering excitation under different wavelength, the emission traps from different oxygenic groups are also changed to emit tunable emission bands. The absence of emission peaks at λ ex < 350 nm or λ ex > 510 nm may attribute to the mismatching energy traps during recombination of excitons and defects [21,22]. PL emission spectra of the CCQDs synthesized at different heating time under same excitation wavelengths (λ ex = 470 nm) is shown in Fig. S2c. It can be seen that PL intensity of the solution at 6 h is the highest, indicating a maximal excitation. The CIE 1931 color coordinates of the CCQDs (Fig. 2b) indicates that the fluorescence color of CCQDs is consistent with PL spectra. Fig. 2c stacks the UV–vis absorption spectra of corresponding CCQDs at diluted aqueous solutions, showing increased absorbency without changing peak positions with decrease of dilution fold. Two characteristic peaks at 227 and 282 nm are related to π→π* of CC [23] and n →π* transition of CO bond [24], respectively. The linear relationship between peak absorption intensity and dilution times for 282 nm suggests the photoabsorption of corresponding CCQDs is independent on their concentrations. As shown in Fig. S2d, the characteristic peak centers are nearly unchanged, whereas the peak intensities of CCQDs vary with prolonged reaction time which is mainly attributed to the increased CCQDs concentration in aqueous solution with increasing of reaction time [25,26]. More CCQDs are created according to nucleation and epitaxial growth mechanism [27]. According to FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2d), the absorption peak at 3385 cm−1 corresponds to OH vibration, while the peak at 1407 cm−1 indicates the presence of COOH groups. The absorption bands at 2933 and 1521 cm−1 are ascribed to CH stretching and CC stretching, respectively. The COC, CO and CH2 groups are also determined in CCQDs at 1077, 872 and 587 cm−1, respectively. The FTIR characterization confirms the decoration of CCQDs by oxygenic species. From optical transmittance spectra in Fig. S2e, one can find that the transparency is zero below 300 nm for the FTO glass supported CCQDs-TiO2 photoanode, suggesting a complete conversion from UV light into electricity. However, the increased transmission at higher wavelengths refers to light loss and the transparency is 54.9% at λ = 700 nm. EDS characterization in Fig. S2f–h indicates that the atomic C/O ratio increases from 44.95/41.82 at a heating time of 2 h to 51.37/35.70 at a heating time of 12 h. The reduced oxygen content is beneficial to form a large conjugated structure.

The CCQDs are used to combine with N719 dye as light harvesters in mesoscopic TiO2 photoanode, and the solar cell device is made according to the layout in Fig. 3 a, comprising of an FTO glass supported CCQDs/N719TiO2, a I−/I3 − redox electrolyte, and an FTO glass supported Pt counter electrode. In fact, the power generation obeys the photoelectric theory under sunlight, as shown in Fig. 3b. By performing cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement (Fig. 3c) [28], the energy levels of HOMO and LUMO of CCQDs synthesized at a reaction time of 6 h are determined to be −5.4 and −4.1 eV below vacuum lever, respectively. The matching HOMO edge (−5.4 eV) of CCQDs to HOMO value (−5.45 eV) of N719 dye and redox potential of I−/I3 − couples (−4.9 eV) demonstrates a bridge for extracting hole from N719 to electrolyte, leaving efficient electron-hole separation and increased voltage output. In addition, the narrow energy bandgap (1.2 eV) of CCQDs makes it possible to absorb NIR light below 1097 nm and to excite electrons to conduction band of TiO2. As shown in Fig. 3d, the MSCs with CCQDs-only harvesters at different heating times yield a maximized η of 0.182% at a heating time of 6 h and the photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 1 . By combining CCQDs with N719 dye, as shown in Fig. 3e, the solar cell achieves efficiency as high as 8.68% in comparison to 7.83% for CCQDs-free device. An increased J sc from 16.4 to 16.6 mA cm−2 and V oc from 0.703 to 0.721 V cross-checked the wide-spectral absorption and fast charge extractions.

EIS Nyquist and Bode phase plots in Fig. 4 a and b are recorded to compare the electrochemical processes of CCQDs-free and CCQDs/N719 tailored devices. Obviously, the MSC with CCQDs/N719 has lower R ct2 at photoanode/electrolyte (11.34 Ω cm2) in comparison with 18.72 Ω cm2 for pristine solar cell, suggesting a reduced recombination reaction and increase charge extraction.

In summary, we demonstrate the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs with cubic shape using Gelidium amansii as raw materials. The MSCs with CCQDs/N719 light-harvesters yield a cell efficiency of 8.68%, which is much higher than 7.83% and 0.182% for N719-only and CCQDs-only photovoltaics, respectively. The potential mechanism behind this enhancement is the widened light absorption as fast charge extraction for efficient electron-hole separation. The finding on CCQDs provides new opportunities of developing extraordinary CQDs, while the increased solar cell efficiency by combing CCQDs with N719 make it possible to further enhance photovoltaic performances of MSCs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21503202 and 61774139), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (JQ201714) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (201762018).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.04.124.
